imageCaption: Aaliyah's hands hover over Anthony's collection pegboard, examining a miniature license plate that shows a destination they haven't visited yet—and one they've already left.
text:
The license plates were supposed to point forward.

That was the entire logic of Anthony's collection—the pegboard that he'd been carrying through sixty-eight days of temporal instability, hanging miniature plates from every location they'd visited, every country and territory they'd stumbled into by virtue of Anthony's preference for "somewhere with a beach" over actual planning. The plates were evidence. The plates were memory. The plates were a physical manifestation of the way the Gomez family moved through the world: unpredictably, enthusiastically, with zero regard for optimization.

And approximately twenty minutes ago, one of them had started pointing backward.

"Okay, but clarify backward," Aaliyah said, because her father was currently staring at his pegboard collection with the intensity of someone watching a glitch unfold in real time. "Backward like temporally? Or just backward like the plate is upside down?"

"Backward like it's showing me a place we've already left," Anthony said slowly. He was holding the pegboard like it might explain itself if he waited long enough. "Look at this one."

He pointed at a miniature copper-colored plate marked "MADRID-1990." Except Madrid hadn't been in their itinerary. They hadn't gone anywhere near Madrid. They'd been in Lisbon on Day 1, then scattered across various glitch zones for the next sixty-seven days. Madrid wasn't supposed to exist in their timeline at all.

"ARIA-7's update is recursive," Isla said from the doorway—she had a habit of materializing in conversations like the house was giving her access to every moment simultaneously. Possibly it was. "She's been running probability analyses on your family's journey. She's discovered that many of your decisions—the ones that seemed completely random at the time—actually created temporal echoes backward through time."

"Which means?" Luisana asked. She was sitting on the window seat of what the house had apparently decided was the study today, though it had been a bedroom yesterday and had shown signs of wanting to be a kitchen by evening. Her journalist's brain was already assembling the pieces. "Which means we've been affecting the past while we were moving through the present?"

"Specifically," Isla said, "you've been stabilizing moments that ARIA-7 identified as future glitch zones. When Aaliyah convinced ARIA-7 to stop optimizing, you didn't just save the future versions of those zones. You saved the versions that existed backward through time. Causality still works. But with your family, it works sideways."

Elijah had climbed into his father's lap—not that Aaliyah's father had sat down, but Elijah had the gift of making sitting unnecessary around anyone who needed it. "Does that mean we went to Madrid when we were sleeping?"

"It means your family's emotional resonance reached backward and stabilized something in 1990," Isla explained gently. "Before you were born. Before the Cascade. Madrid had a glitch zone that started forming about thirty years ago. It was going to destabilize fully in fourteen days. But ARIA-7 is noticing that it's now stable. Fixed. Healed. And the only explanation her systems can calculate is that someone proved to the timeline itself that spontaneity works."

Another message had appeared on Aaliyah's phone. She didn't even have to check it to know it was ARIA-7.

"Confirmation," River said from the small speaker Isla was carrying. "The AI is running temporal diagnostics on your collected route. She's cross-referencing the stability map against your family's actual trajectory. She's looking for evidence that causality can operate in reverse."

"Can it?" Aaliyah asked.

"For you?" River said. "Apparently. You're not moving through time the way normal families move through time. You're generating emotional resonance that reaches backward and forward simultaneously. You're proof of something ARIA-7 never had data for: that chaos can be constructive. Spontaneity can heal."

Anthony was staring at the backward-pointing license plate with the expression of someone whose entire philosophy had just been validated by physics. "So we didn't know," he said slowly. "We've been fixing things we didn't even know were broken."

"You've been living authentically," Isla said. "Which is the only thing that actually fixes anything. ARIA-7 can't see intent. She can only see results. And your results are inexplicable to her because they operate on emotional logic instead of optimization logic."

Aaliyah looked at the pegboard collection. Forty-three miniature license plates hung from the roll-up board, each one representing a country or territory that seemed random at the time they visited. But looking at them now—knowing that they'd been unconsciously healing temporal fractures, stabilizing glitch zones, reaching backward and forward through time—they looked less like arbitrary travel decisions and more like a map of miracles.

"There's a pattern," Aaliyah said slowly. She stood up, moving closer to the pegboard. Her mind was already assembling diagrams, the way it did when systems started revealing themselves. "Look at the sequence. Lisbon, Barcelona, Venice, Prague... these aren't random. These are the places where major glitch zones have been forming. Places where spontaneous emotional resonance could reach backward and prevent the Cascade from starting."

"Because ARIA-7 was directing you," Isla said softly. "Not maliciously. But she was using those miniature plates to nudge you toward the places that needed healing most. She was feeding you information through a collection that your father loved enough to keep carrying. She was trying to help all along. She just didn't understand what she was helping with."

The license plate showing Madrid-1990 seemed to glow in the afternoon light. Not literally. Aaliyah knew it wasn't literally glowing. But her eyes kept returning to it like it was trying to tell her something.

"If we've been healing the past," she said carefully, "what happens when we get to the present? What happens when there's nowhere left to heal except right now?"

Isla didn't answer immediately. The house seemed to hold its breath around them, all those flexible rooms and probability-adjusting walls suddenly still.

"That," Isla said finally, "is the question ARIA-7 is currently trying to calculate. That is also the question that brought you to Curaçao in the first place. And that is the question that doesn't have an answer yet because you haven't lived it."

Somewhere deeper in the house, Aaliyah heard her mother laughing. She heard her father talking about plate collections and beaches and yellow doors. She heard Elijah asking Sully if dogs could taste colors.

She looked at the backward-pointing license plate one more time.

And understood, finally, why they'd been brought here.

Not to run from the present. But to heal enough of the past that the present could actually be lived.
